Therapist: You've had a positive experience where focusing on something else, like traffic, helped you forget about smoking. Would you be willing to try using a similar distraction, like paying attention to your surroundings, while you're in situations where you normally smoke?

Patient: I think it's worth a try. I could listen to the radio or podcasts while I have my break. Something to keep my mind occupied. That way, when I get to the point where I normally smoke, I might not even notice I'm craving it.

Therapist: You're already thinking of ways to keep your mind occupied, like listening to the radio or podcasts. How might you make this a regular habit, something you can turn to whenever you feel the urge to smoke?

Patient: I could make a playlist or something. Put it on my phone and just press play when I need to. And maybe take a few minutes to stretch or move around, get the blood flowing. That might help distract me too.
